title: Groundbreaking Aussie test 'picked up 100 per cent of early ovarian cancers'
text:
Queensland researchers are developing a potentially world-changing screening test for ovarian cancer , one of the most serious cancers affecting women.

Currently, less than half of women diagnosed with ovarian cancer will be alive five years later, and there is no screening program despite survival rates being much higher when the cancer is detected early.

Scientists from the University of Queensland have developed a screening test based on﻿ microscopic bubbles, called extracellular vesicles (EVs), which are naturally released by cells and carry protein and genetic information that can signal the presence of cancer.

Professor Carlos Salomon Gallo (University of Queensland)

The test demonstrated 100 per cent detection of early-stage (I and II) ovarian cancer across more than 500 blood samples.

Professor Carlos Salomon Gallo, the director of the UQ Centre for Extracellular Vesicle Nanomedicine said two out of three women who develop ovarian cancer are not diagnosed until the disease is advanced, when treatment becomes more complex and survival rates poor.

"Rapid and accurate identification of asymptomatic ovarian cancer will dramatically improve survival rates," Salomon Gallo said.

"Another advantage of this test is the extremely low false positive rate of 0.4 per cent in recent studies, which makes it a potential candidate as a screening tool."

UQ has licensed the technology to ASX-listed biotech company INOVIQ Ltd and is now studying the impact of shipment and storage on the stability of the biomarkers in the samples.

"Our aim is to systematically evaluate the impact of blood collection protocols, storage temperatures, transport conditions and time-to-processing on EV biomarker stability," Salomon Gallo said.

"This will allow us to establish a standardised specimen handling protocol for the test under real-world conditions."

The screening test is based on extracellular vesicles, microscopic bubbles that are naturally released by cells. (University of Queensland)

Researchers are looking for postmenopausal women between the ages of 50 and 74 with intact ovaries, and no prior history of ovarian cancer to enrol in a study .

Professor Andreas Obermair, a surgeon who works with women with ovarian cancer, said we "urgently need to change" the amount of women being diagosed when the cancer was already advanced.

"This study relies on women volunteering to donate a small blood sample and some basic health information - it is quick, safe and free," she said.

Ovarian Cancer Foundation CEO Robin Penty said the research was a "significant milestone" towards early detection and screening.

"The study represents real progress and future hope for everyone affected by this insidious disease, so we are very excited to see the results."

The Ovarian Cancer Research Foundation donated more than $1.7 million to fund Gallo's work.

The project received grants by the National Health and Medical Research Council (NHMRC) in partnership with the Ovarian Cancer Research Foundation and the Lions Medical Research Foundation, and in collaboration with Mater Research andQueensland Centre for Gynaecological Cancer Research.

title: 'Tax on households' coming for Aussies due to Iran war
text:
The Reserve Bank has cautioned it remains too early to tell what the economic impact of the war in Iran will be, even as surging petrol prices deliver another hit to inflation-weary households.

Speaking at the Australian Financial Review's Business Summit in Sydney this morning, RBA Governor Michele Bullock was characteristically guarded when addressing the outbreak of conflict in the Middle East over the weekend.

She said while a disruption of oil and gas supplies could send Australia's already-high inflation further in the wrong direction, a prolonged conflict could have the opposite effect, albeit while dragging the rest of the economy down with it.

LIVE UPDATES: Iran says it will attack ships on key oil route ﻿

Michele Bullock says it's too early to say what the economic impact of the war in Iran will be. (Dominic Lorrimer)

"It's too early to say what the economic impact will be," she said.

"Events are moving rapidly and there are different ways this can play out. We will take some time to make sense of what it could mean for inflation here…

"It is not obvious how this might play out."

The most obvious impact for Australians so far has been at the petrol pump.

In NSW, for example, the average price for a litre of unleaded 91 has jumped from 178.7 cents on Friday to 185.5 cents today.

One servo, according to state government data, is already slugging motorists 256.9 cents a litre.

Surging petrol prices are already hitting Australian households. (Dion Georgopoulos)

This is despite the NRMA saying yesterday that it should take 7-10 days for surging oil prices to flow through to the bowser.

"In Sydney, Melbourne and Brisbane, we are now at the top of the price cycle, and if anything prices should start to come down," spokesperson Peter Khoury said.

While Iran is a significant oil producer, the far greater issue is the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, corking a crucial bottleneck where one-fifth of the world's supply passes through.

Iran's Revolutionary Guards have now threatened to strike any ships that pass through the critical strategic passage between Iran and Oman, which at its narrowest is just 39km wide.

The Strait of Hormuz stretches for 39km between Iran and Oman, and is a critical waterway for global oil supplies. (Getty)

Oil prices rose to $US77.60 per barrel yesterday, but analysts have predicted it could hit as high as $US100 – shy of the $US125 reached following Russia's invasion of Ukraine, but still well above the roughly $US60 it was selling for at the start of the year.

If that plays out – and with US President Donald Trump talking about a weeks-long operation and refusing to rule out putting troops on the ground, there clearly is a chance of a long, drawn out-conflict – it will be a significant hit to Australian households.﻿

"Roughly speaking each $US1 a barrel rise in oil prices adds around a cent a litre to petrol prices," AMP chief economist Shane Elliott said.

"So, a $US40 a barrel rise in world oil prices taking them above $US100 a barrel would add around 40 cents a litre with a 7-10 day lag if sustained.

There are signs the conflict could evolve into a long, drawn-out war. (AP)

"A 40 cents a litre rise in petrol prices would add about 0.8 per cent to CPI inflation, but it would also impart a dampening impact on growth.

"This is because it would add around $14 a week to the household petrol bill leading to a cut-back in spending elsewhere in the economy.

"In other words, it will act as a tax on households."

While he argued the RBA should look through that inflation spike, Bullock admitted today her bank might not be able to ignore a temporary shock to the system, with inflation already far too high for anyone's liking.

"That's a very relevant point," she said.

"With a supply shock occurring in a situation where we already have high inflation, I think there is a risk that inflation expectations may start to move.

"That will be something, I think, that will be front of mind for the (monetary policy) board."

That, in turn, could raise the chance of an interest rate hike, which many economists already think is highly likely in May.

title: Dramatic moment truckie plucked to safety in outback floods
text:
A truck driver has been winched to safety from his cabin as floodwaters engulfed the vehicle south-west of Broken Hill.

Rain has inundated outback NSW, closing highways from Queensland to South Australia.

The rescue helicopter arrived in the remote town of Yunta near Broken Hill.

One crew member was winched down, balancing on top of the cabin as the driver clung on.He attached the harness and both were brought to safety (Nine)

He attached the harness and both were brought to safety.

title: Australian agriculture hits $100 billion target ahead of schedule
text:
The value of Australia's farming produce is tipped to reach its "ambitious" $100 billion goal this financial year, but experts warn the value could dip again in the face of trying trade and seasonal conditions.

The head of the National Farmers Federation (NFF), Hamish McIntryre, described it as a "landmark moment for Australian agriculture".

"In 2018, we set a bold target to grow Australian agriculture into a $100 billion industry by 2030," he said.

"At the time, some thought it was ambitious and some didn't think we stood a chance.

"But farmers wouldn't be in this game if they weren't up for a challenge."

The 2018–19 farmgate value was around $60 billion, amid worsening drought conditions.

Announcing the latest figures at the Australian Bureau of Agricultural and Resource Economics and Sciences (ABARES) annual conference, Mr McIntyre said the last few years had not always been easy.

" This milestone wasn't handed to us, but earned through droughts, fires and floods, through a pandemic and global trade disruption. "

Hamish McIntyre says the milestone is a "landmark moment". (ABC News: Lydia Burton)

Australia last year celebrated a record year for Australian beef exports, despite US President Donald Trump's tariffs.

It was also the second-largest winter crop harvest on record, with new technologies key in helping drought-hit farmers still get something in the bins.

Mr McIntyre, who runs a family livestock and cropping operation in southern Queensland, said the goal was "never just about chasing a headline number".

"It is underpinned by farmer wellbeing, sustainability and natural capital alongside productivity and profitability," he said.

"There's no doubt about our scientists, our engineers, and everyone involved is part of this.

"Our climate's always variable, so being able to produce gross margins from our paddocks, from our farms, no matter what's going on with the climate, is critical and science is helping drive that."

Federal Agriculture Minister Julie Collins thanked Australia's farmers for their "hard work, resilience and skill".

"Being on track to exceed the industry's $100 billion target by four years is a tremendous result for our farmers and regional communities," she said.

Ms Collins said diversifying trade opportunities was an ongoing focus of the federal government.

Australian beef exports have soared despite Donald Trump's tariffs. (ABC Landline)

Tougher year ahead

While the $100 billion headline figure is grand, the reality amid global trade tensions and impacts of war, as well as trying seasonal conditions, is that next financial year the value is expected to dip.

ABARES executive director Dr Jared Greenville said the value of the current financial year reflected higher crop export volumes, and higher overall livestock and livestock product export prices.

"Following the strong performance this year, we're expecting production to ease in 2026–27, with below-average rainfall expected for much of southern Australia in autumn," Dr Greenville said.

" Crop prices are also expected to decline in 2026–27 as prices in global markets for many crops decline. "

Dr Greenville predicts next year's farmgate value to dip to around $95 billion, which is still historically high.

"Challenges in market access and uncertainty over recent trade announcements will weigh on export prices for livestock," he said.

ABARES says the national 2025–26 winter crop was the second-largest on record. (ABC Rural: Amelia Bernasconi)

Mr McIntyre acknowledged the new Middle Eastern conflict would add extra complexities for the final months of this financial year.

"It is critical that government and industry work closely together.

"At the moment, it'll be very hard to ship to the Middle East with what's going on over there, so we need to be able to move these commodities to other markets."

Mr McIntyre said other threats to the ongoing growth of the industry came back to labour and housing affordability in regional areas.

"We also need vibrant, healthy communities that have schools and hospitals and everything families want and need to encourage them to come back to regional Australia," he said.

" We at NFF now will consider our next goal and ambition, but today, we're celebrating. "

title: Scott welcomes Bruhn return after 'egregious injustice' of false rape allegation
text:
Geelong's Tanner Bruhn is set for his first AFL game in 17 months after suffering the "egregious" injustice of being falsely accused of rape, his coach says.

Bruhn will play for the Cats in their season-opener against Gold Coast on Friday night after missing last season while facing criminal charges.

Bruhn was stood down by the AFL when charged with rape and intentional sexual touching. Those charges were dropped last November when a key witness admitted to lying.

Prosecutors drop sexual assault, rape charges against Tanner Bruhn Lawyers for Geelong AFL player Tanner Bruhn say "false allegations" were made against him and a friend, telling the media the pair have been vindicated in court.

Geelong coach Chris Scott says Bruhn will play against the Suns — his first AFL game since the 2024 preliminary final.

"It's just fantastic for him to get out there and be able to concentrate on footy after what is one of the most egregious injustices I can think of in the game," Scott told reporters on Tuesday.

"So to put that behind him, I think, is positive for everyone."

Scott was reluctant to detail the support he offered to the 23-year-old Bruhn, who has played 66 AFL games since his debut in 2021 at GWS. He transferred to Geelong at the end of the 2022 season.

"I've said more than I wanted to anyway, it's largely a private matter and I don't want to draw any more attention to it than he would like," he said.

" I'd support him in all the ways that you would imagine but I don't want to walk you through it. "

Scott trialled Bruhn at half-back instead of his customary midfield position during the pre-season.

"It's his first game [back] but he's really been training and he played a practice game against Carlton, he's kind of ready to go," he said.

"So I think we'll all look back and think this was a small step — it's important that we don't overplay it."

Scott confirmed aces Jeremy Cameron and Bailey Smith would feature in the away game against the Suns after battling thigh and calf ailments respectively in the pre-season.

Jeremy Cameron is set to feature for Geelong despite an interrupted pre-season. (AAP: James Ross )

Forward/ruck Shannon Neale would also front after copping a knock to a shin in the last pre-season trial, but forward Gryan Miers (thumb) remained in doubt.

"We don't have a very high tolerance for pushing guys that aren't ready," Scott said.

"We're much less tolerant of that stuff now … he (Miers) had surgery on a thumb, he has got the all-clear to play but just because he can play doesn't mean he should.

"Gryan, it's one of those situations where 15 years ago he would be a certainty … because it was a badge of honour playing with these things."

AAP

title: Coastal growers take on Sturt's desert pea as demand for natives soars
text:
Nestled within the lush green bushland of the NSW Mid North Coast, Paul Dalley's flower farm is the last place a crop of Sturt's desert pea would be expected to thrive.

The floral emblem of South Australia normally grows in the outback, in arid conditions and sandy soil, but Mr Dalley is growing the flower in a greenhouse in Kempsey, where dozens of the native desert flowers are in full bloom.

Before Mr Dalley attempted to grow the flower last year, he had only seen it once, at Melbourne's botanic gardens.

"We just like growing things that are beautiful and different, and nobody else is growing," he said.

"They're beautiful, they're unique, a lot of people have seen pictures but have never seen them for real."

Paul Dalley loves the challenge of unique Australian blooms. (ABC Rural: Lauren Bohane)

Mr Dalley had about 40 plants in this year's crop, each sporting several flowers.

"We're doing much better than last year when most of the plants died off, and we only got about half a dozen flowers from 10 plants," he said.

But he said the challenge was what drew him to unusual flowers.

Paul Dalley plans to collect seeds from this year's crop of Sturt's desert pea. (ABC News: Lauren Bohane)

"Some of our crops are more on the mainstream side, and some are more far out, and some of them fail altogether.

" It's a bit like gambling, but I don't gamble, so I buy exotic plants and seeds. "

Mr Dalley said there had been massive interest in the flowers, but he planned to save most of this year's crop for seeds.

In South Australia, Sturt's desert pea is considered a protected flora, meaning flowers and plants cannot be collected from private or Crown land without permission.

Growing interest in natives

Further south, on the NSW Central Coast, East Coast Wildflowers' Jonathan Lidbetter has been growing Sturt's desert pea for eight years.

"It normally grows in areas of rainfall of about 250-500mm a year, and we routinely get over 1,100mm, so we must be mad," Mr Lidbetter said.

"We've had various quality issues with seed and haven't made it a huge focus until the last couple of years.

"In the last couple of years, we've really tried hard to grow it better, to make it more commercial and to make the product desirable for the customer."

Jonathan Lidbetter has been growing Sturt's desert pea for eight years. (Supplied: Jonathan Lidbetter)

The blooms were sold at Sydney Flower Markets, where Mr Lidbetter said there was growing interest in native flowers.

"There's a move to more interesting, delicate flowers that have not really been available before," Mr Lidbetter said.

"COVID partly brought in a little bit of interest in locally-grown native flowers [with] short flower miles."

Sturt's desert pea is notoriously difficult to grow outside of its native habitat. (ABC News: Lauren Bohane)

Mr Lidbetter said limited availability kept florists coming back to see what was in season.

"Seasonality keeps the florists interested in the market area because they're always coming in to see what's new, what's hitting the floor.

"A lot of their traditional flowers that they're used to, they're able to get 12 months a year, and they can just order them.

" A lot of the things that are now coming along in the native market line are very restricted supplies and often quite unusual. "

Natives not always Australian-grown

The peak body for Australian flower growers and florists credited the rise in popularity of native flowers to Australians' growing desire to buy local.

"A lot of people in Australia relate them to being Australian-grown products, and there's a real trend towards wanting to buy and support Australian-grown flowers and Aussie businesses," Flower Industry Australia secretary Anna Jabour said.

Anna Jabour says native flowers are becoming more popular. (Supplied: Flower Industry Australia)

However, Ms Jabour warned Australian native did not always equate to Australian-grown when it came to flowers.

" I've seen kangaroo paw [and] some eucalyptus be imported, and that's why it's really important to ask where your flowers are from. "

She said the flower industry had been advocating for mandated country-of-origin labelling for the past two decades.

"If there's no labelling telling people where flowers are from, a lot of the Australian public think that the flowers are just grown here," she said.

"Twenty-five years ago, that assumption would be correct; around 90 per cent or more of our flowers were grown here, but today it's only around 50 per cent."

title: Queensland government considers reform after inheriting 'non-compliant' fire truck fleet
text:
Queensland volunteer firefighters say they are being sent to fire grounds in vehicles that fail to meet basic crew safety requirements.

A right to information request by the Rural Fire Brigades Association Queensland (RFBAQ) found more than 250 complaints were made to the state government about its new fleet of fire trucks within just 10 months last year.

Issues reported by volunteers included gearbox failures which left trucks immobile, water pumps stopping unexpectedly and hose reels becoming loose or falling from vehicles.

RFBAQ general manager Justin Choveaux said the right to information request was made after a crew became stuck in a north Queensland bushfire last year.

Justin Choveaux says the Rural Fire Brigade Association feels "ignored". (ABC News: Amy Sheehan)

He said his organisation wanted a review of the state government's vehicle procurement processes because volunteers were not involved.

"We're sending [firefighters] out there in vehicles that we know fail repeatedly on basic crew safety matters," Mr Choveaux said.

The Queensland Fire Department (QFD) ordered about 80 "next generation" medium trucks from Victorian-based company Bell Environmental in 2022 to replace aging equipment, but Mr Choveaux said his organisation was not consulted on the designs.

"Vehicles then started to go into production without any look or feel had by the brigade volunteers," he said.

"We said this was a hazard, and the fire service ignored us."

Justin Choveaux (second from left) fighting a fire for the Rural Fire Brigade in 2017. (Supplied: Justin Choveaux)

Purchase followed 'strict' policy

In response the Queensland Fire Department said in a statement that each vehicle was given "quality assurance checks" before going to brigades and that fault reports were reviewed "as a priority".

QFD said areas for immediate improvement were identified after trucks were put into service and it worked with the manufacturers to fix any faults.

About 60 medium attack fire trucks have been delivered to rural stations across Queensland. (Supplied: Queensland Fire Department)

The department said it handled all fleet and equipment purchases in accordance with "strict" Queensland government policies.

A spokesperson for Minister for Fire, Disaster Recovery and Volunteers, Ann Leahy, said the government was looking at making changes to vehicle procurement that included involving volunteer firefighters in the process.

The minister's office also said it was working through a "laundry list of Labor's failures" which included "non-compliant vehicles".

National park blaze sparks investigation

Mr Choveaux said the RFBAQ information request followed an incident which left rural volunteers immobilised during a bushfire in Cape Palmerston, 88 kilometres south of Mackay, in October 2025.

"Habana Rural Fire Brigade… they had a hose reel fall off their truck," he said.

"They got onto us and they said, 'This isn't good enough.'"

Hundreds of complaints have been made about the medium attack fire trucks. (AAP Image: Patrick Hamilton)

The incident resulted in QFD issuing a 'safety recall advice' for volunteers to inspect the next generation vehicles for loose bolts.

QFD said it encouraged brigades to report suggestions for "design improvements or emerging issues".

Bell Environmental was contacted for comment.

title: Storm left with huge legal costs as Lomax saga reaches shock conclusion
text:
The Parramatta Eels have declared the club won't be "steamrolled" after star winger Zac Lomax's audacious bid to join NRL rival Melbourne was blocked.

In a shock end to the sport's biggest off-season saga, the Storm and Eels reached an out-of-court settlement on Tuesday morning that gives Parramatta the power to determine whether Lomax can return to the NRL before the 2028 season.

The Eels remain open to striking a deal that would permit the NSW State of Origin winger to play with an NRL rival in 2026 and 2027, but only if they receive adequate compensation.

That could involve the Eels receiving a similarly elite player, who would need to consent to being traded for Lomax, or significant salary cap compensation from that rival club.

Parramatta Eels CEO Jim Sarantinos says the club won't be 'steamrolled' in negotations over Lomax's exit. (AAP Photos: Bianca De Marchi)

The Storm will be required to pay $250,000 of Parramatta's legal costs over their failed bid to lure Lomax, who had agreed not to join an NRL rival without the Eels' consent when the club released him to join R360 in November.

When the launch of the renegade rugby competition was postponed, Lomax began advances to join the Storm, triggering the Eels' legal action in the NSW Supreme Court.

"I think we've demonstrated through this process we're not going to be steamrolled," Eels chief executive Jim Sarantinos said.

Melbourne will foot their own legal bills and those of Lomax, who did not appear in court on Tuesday as the NRL clubs told Justice Francois Kunc they would not require a full-scale court hearing.

The Storm refused to offer up a high-profile player such as Xavier Coates (pictured) to the Eels in exchange for Lomax. (Getty Images: Cameron Spencer)

Lomax will at least be permitted to return to the NRL without the Eels' consent for the 2028 season, a year earlier than initially agreed when he left Parramatta one year into a four-year deal.

The Eels were blindsided in late December to learn of Lomax's contract talks with the Storm but spent weeks working with the club on a deal that could've permitted the 26-year-old to join Melbourne.

The Storm had refused to swap Lomax for one of their own high-profile players in Jack Howarth, Stefano Utoikamanu or Xavier Coates, while the Eels knocked back a $300,000 payment.

Mr Sarantinos stressed the Eels held no personal grudge against Lomax, but they would not willingly strengthen a rival club without recompense.

"This was never about Zac personally, Zac's a good young man," he said.

"We don't have any concerns about Zac playing in the NRL, we obviously want him to be able to move on with his life. But this is about … standing up for what's right for our club."

Eels head coach Jason Ryles and general manager of football Mark O'Neill will be the key men in deciding whether a proposed trade represents fair value for Parramatta.

The Eels are unlikely to entertain a transfer fee if the money cannot be put into the salary cap used to sign players.

The Eels and Storm face each other in round of the NRL season on Thursday in a spicy post-script to the saga.

Eels coach Jason Ryles (pictured) will have a big say in what the club accepts in exchange for Lomax. (Getty Images: Matt Blyth)

"It's a bit of rugby league poetry, isn't it?" Mr Sarantinos said, saying a win in Melbourne would be "a nice way to wrap this whole thing up".

Outside of the NRL, a switch to rugby union remains an option for Lomax.

While in limbo, he turned down offers to join Australia's Super Rugby competition, where he would've earned significantly less than his $700,000 Eels salary.

An overseas rugby union move could prove more lucrative, though Lomax has not played the 15-man code since childhood.

AAP

title: Broome does not have accessible taxis, so one local is providing free rides
text:
When resort owner Andy Guidera first moved to a tourism hotspot in Western Australia's far north, he used the wheelchair taxi service nearly every day.

Thirty years later, the service is no longer available in Broome, 2,080 kilometres north of Perth.

"It seems like in some areas we've gone backwards," Mr Guidera said.

Andy Guidera says older people and people with disability should be well looked after in every community. (ABC Kimberley: Rachel Jackson)

Taking care of the community

The only taxi business providing wheelchair-accessible services in the Kimberley region is located in Derby, more than 200km east of Broome.

For the past six years, Mr Guidera said he had offered people in need of the service lifts using his own vehicle, free of charge.

"I know how difficult it is for them to travel and to get up and get out and about in the first place," he said.

Mr Guidera said while he could not drive the van himself, it was operated by his staff and fitted out with a platform and equipment to make it accessible for people using wheelchairs.

He said the absence of accessible taxis was unwelcoming for tourists and residents, and he hoped an operator would open a service in Broome in future.

"The older people and the disabled people in every community should be well looked after, especially in a first world country like Australia," he said.

'Really frustrating'

WA Greens senator and disability advocate Jordon Steele-John said the absence of wheelchair accessible services was "just not OK".

Simple tasks including grocery shopping, meeting friends and travelling to medical appointments were not possible for a person to do independently without an accessible taxi, he said.

Jordon Steele-John says the lack of wheelchair-accessible taxi services stops people from doing everyday tasks. (Supplied: Jordon Steele-John)

"It sends the message to people that they just don't seem to matter the same amount to government and to service providers, and it's really frustrating," the senator said.

Operators can apply for state government grants of up to $80,000 to help install wheelchair accessible taxis in regions where the service is not already available.

The latest recipients of the annual funding included businesses in Exmouth and Port Hedland.

A state government spokesperson said the Department of Transport and Major Infrastructure (DTMI) had not received any applications from Broome taxi operators.

Operators facilitate 'what they can'

Operators in Broome have cited high costs, low demand and limited staff as the reasons behind their decision not to apply for the grant.

Broome Private Taxis owner Paul Rewell said installing the wheelchair taxi services was not viable for his business.

"It's not a decision we take lightly," he said.

"It's about the fact that we run a very tight ship here … and it's still a pretty expensive job."

Paul Rewell says installing wheelchair-accessible taxis is not viable for his business. (ABC Kimberley: Rachel Jackson)

Mr Rewell said his business also provided transport to people who had capacity to move in and out of their wheelchair, which could then be folded in the back.

"We try to facilitate as much as we can, where we can, but you can't fill every service that's required all the time," he said.

Mr Rewell said increasing the grant and subsidy would help to incentivise more operators to provide the service, but he also suggested the government invest in a dedicated driver.

Grant available

Shire of Broome President Chris Mitchell said he welcomed any grants supporting operators to install the service, but these needed to be tailored to the region.

Chris Mitchell says wheelchair-accessible taxi grants must suit individual regions. (ABC Kimberley: Andy Seabourne)

"A one-size-fits-all model isn't effective. The government's got to come up with something that benefits Broome," he said.

A DTMI spokesperson said the accessible taxi grants program was reviewed annually.

"For the 2025 to 2026 round, the department prioritised areas without an operational wheelchair accessible taxi service to ensure funding is targeted at areas with the greatest need, one of those areas being Broome," they said.

"DTMI will continue to work closely with prospective applicants from all areas to make sure they understand the grant requirements and how to apply, helping to ensure the application process is clear and accessible."

title: How gen alpha's fascination with Asian culture will shape Australia's future
text:
Australia's cultural embrace of Asia — from Korean pop music and beauty regimens to Asian food and Eastern medicine — is expected to swell with the rise of generation alpha.

Brisbane-based futurist Tony Hunter says Australia will become increasingly influenced by the generation of people born between 2010 and 2024.

Futurists analyse social trends, emerging technologies, demographics and other broad influences to understand how the future may unfold.

Hunter says generation alpha are unique in a particular way.

Join us this weekend at BrisAsia Stories ABC Radio Brisbane is proud to be the official Media Partner for BrisAsia Festival, connecting community through culture.

"Generation alpha are not just digital natives, but technology natives," he says.

"They have technology in nearly everything they do, particularly in advanced countries."

Hunter says trying to understand generation alpha in the future will be important in understanding social change in Australia.

"[It] won't be a matter of simply understanding Australian content, but also content from India and from South-East Asia and China," he says.

Gen alpha rising

Generation alpha is the world's largest generation, currently 2 billion in number, according to data and research firm Intelpoint.

Demon Hunters help drive 'explosion' in K-pop in Australia About 200 performers will take the stage for the K-pop showcase at this weekend's BrisAsia festival.

Aged between 2 and 16, we mainly know gen alpha for their memes, prompting their parents to ask, "What does Skibidi mean?" or, "What's rizz?"

As gen alpha ages into adulthood, their views on what to love and loathe will shape all of us.

From work to religion, leisure to social etiquette — we can expect the ideas that take hold of them to spread through social media.

So what else do we know about them?

The top three populations of generation alpha around the world are India, China and Indonesia, with Nigeria set to have the third largest population by 2050.

India has 390 million generation alphas, while China has 230 million, according to research by IFF and Daxue Consulting.

Indonesia has 65 million and the Philippines has 27.6 million. 78 million are in Nigeria.

In contrast, Australia has 3 million, the United Kingdom has 9.2 million, and the United States has 45.6 million.

According to Brookings, by 2045 in the US, it's estimated that the population will be 24.6 per cent Hispanic, 13.1 per cent black and 7.9 per cent Asian.

So, the traditional influences on Australian culture — an Anglo-centric UK and US, will become more varied, and be joined by larger populations of youth from Asia.

We're already starting to see signs.

This year's Superbowl included a Spanish-language-heavy half-time performance by Bad Bunny.

A recent trend of "Chinamaxxing" has emerged in generation Z.

It includes trying to incorporate Chinese elements into your life, from drinking warm water, to using bone broth for breakfast.

A variety of foods were on offer at the BrisAsia Festival. (Supplied: Brisbane City Council)

They follow recent interest in K-beauty (Korean beauty) tips, which have brought salmon sperm and water sleeping masks into the mainstream.

There will be a rapid expansion of Asian content creators, fuelled by the adoption of smartphones in emerging markets across South and South East Asia.

Kitchen influence

As a sheer numbers game, Hunter agrees social media content from India, South-East Asia and China will become increasingly influential in Australia.

Asian flavours are an evolving part of everyday cooking — including recently adopted trends such as dragon fruit and gochujang, the popular fermented chilli paste from Korea.

Cooking shows have normalised once-exotic ingredients and social media has made it even easier to find food inspiration from Asia.

Chef, author and spice enthusiast Sarina Kamini says Australians are increasingly adopting Asian spices as their own.

"My experience has been that folks really get into the use of spices once they've learned how to adapt an aromatic to the kind of cooking that they love," she says.

"I'm not talking about fusion, exactly. I'm thinking about getting cosy using turmeric and cumin seed in your scrambled eggs.

"And having tamarind concentrate feel as familiar as soy sauce.

"I think that's where spices will have an influence — tricking up the every-night meals of families across Australia."

Australian sales of kimchi, tamarind and other staples of the Asian pantry that are used for flavour and general health are projected to rise in the next 10 to 15 years.

Changing melodies

BrisAsia executive producer Anthony Garcia — a world-renowned guitarist and composer — believes Australia will increasingly hear from artists and groups blending Asian and Western musical instruments.

"We are seeing a rise of fusion groups in Australia, but our mainstream musicians have remained wedded to a Western idea of how pop music should sound," Garcia says.

With large Asian diaspora populations living here, and musicians with the creativity to make ancient Asian instruments palatable to a pop audience, will we see Australian mainstream musicians tap into this?

Microtones, the range of notes between the main notes of an octave, are used widely in Indian and Middle Eastern music to create complex melodies.

Garcia says they open up a new way of playing.

"Microtonal music allows for a nuance of melodic expression that other systems don't have," he says.

"Many sounds in nature, such as birdsong, have a diverse melodic range that goes beyond diatonic or chromatic harmony.

"Microtonal music seems to be able to emulate those natural sounds, which I find really interesting.

"Indian classical music also uses complex rhythms, and we're seeing more Western bands inspired by these and applying them to their own compositions."

For them to become normalised, however, he says music education has to embrace and teach them.

"Children who learn these ideas at school are more likely to use them as adults," he says.

The tones of Asian instruments may also be used to provide new moods to Australian songwriting.

Garcia has been particularly captured by the guqin, a Chinese string instrument that features in the Oceans Art Orchestra.

Nicole Yang (left) rehearses with a guqin with Anthony Garcia. (ABC Radio Brisbane: Ashwin Segkar)

"When I hear the sound, I am transported," he says.

"The instrument is literally 4,000 years old.

"You feel that rich old tone and see what it can do when it's blended in new ways."

Now residing in Brisbane, Thai musician Peranya Visitchantaragoon had her music featured on Season 3 of TV show White Lotus.

She expresses a similar desire to make the old new.

"I would like to bring the old, traditional songs back, remake them and introduce them to everyone in the multicultural community we have here," she says.

"We try to make our music joyful and easy for people to listen to from every background."

She says cross-cultural music has some challenges.

"You have to learn to listen to each other closely and sometimes learn new scales," she says.

Peranya Visitchantaragoon wants traditional music to be shared more widely. (ABC Radio Brisbane: Ashwin Segkar)

Visitchantaragoon says she loves it and improvisation is her favourite part "because you create something that didn't exist before, it's like a type of magic, and those moments feel very alive and honest".

Migration plays a role

The Asian influence on Australia will be driven by the generation alpha conversation that occurs online, but migration will also play a key part.

University of Queensland demographer associate professor Elin Charles-Edwards says that Australia's migration program has been geared towards what is seen as practical work.

"There is definitely a focus on professional occupations," Dr Charles-Edwards says.

"There are special talent visas for artists and musicians if they have a global profile, but it is based on professions.

"This was the bargain that the Australian government made with the Australian people to have a migration program focused around those skills that were seen as practically beneficial."

The children of nurses and pharmacists, of course, occasionally embrace a career in the arts.

The local audiences they sing, joke, write or paint for in the next 10 to 20 years will be more tapped into Asia than ever before.

title: Top runner calls for action after being guided off course during half marathon
text:
A runner is calling for USA Track and Field (USATF) to take action after a mistake by a guide vehicle at the US half marathon championships cost her a spot in the world road running championships.

Jess McClain was leading the women's race by a wide margin with about 2.4 kilometres to go on the weekend when she and three other runners followed the guide vehicle on a wrong turn.

McClain said in an Instagram post that she followed a police escort, official lead vehicle and a media motorcycle off the course for about 1km.

"I had to come to a stop, make a tight and complete u-turn and run back onto course as a national championship title and a world team spot slipped away," she wrote.

"I'm going try my hardest to walk away from this weekend remembering the joy I felt in those moments where I thought I was on my way to becoming a national champion and finally make Team USA outright."

McClain ended up finishing ninth. The next two runners who followed her off the course — Emma Hurley and Ednah Kurgat — were 12th and 13th, respectively.

Molly Born crossed the finish line first in 1 hour, 9 minutes, 43 seconds.

"I don't really feel like the US champion just because of the whole situation that went down at the end," Born said in an interview after the race.

McClain and other runners who followed the lead vehicle appealed after their protest of the order of finish was rejected.

USATF said in a statement the jury of appeals found: "The event did not meet USATF Rule 243 and that the course was not adequately marked at the point of misdirection.

"This violation contributed to the misdirection taken by the athletes within the top four at the time of misdirection.

"However, the jury of appeals finds no recourse within the USATF rulebook to alter the results order of finish. The results order of finish as posted is considered final."

The top three finishers qualify for the world championships on September 20 in Copenhagen, Denmark. The USATF noted that the American team would not be officially selected until May and that the governing body would continue to review what took place in Atlanta.

"While we understand athletes are eager to resolve this issue expeditiously, our process will ensure an ultimate decision is in the best interest of all the athletes involved," the USATF said.

The USATF said lead vehicles were provided and managed by the local organising committee, which was the Atlanta Track Club.

"Mistakes happen and I am sure those who were leading us feel terrible about the outcome," McClain wrote.

"I just hate that the athletes are ALWAYS the ones who pay the price (literally $$$) … time and time again."

She said she trusted that USATF and the Atlanta Track Club would "somehow make it right for the athletes who were led off course".

"It sounds like conversations are still being had, so I am choosing to remain hopeful that we'll soon have a little more clarity than what we left Atlanta with and actionable steps forward."

The ABC of SPORT Sports content to make you think... or allow you not to. A newsletter delivered each Saturday.

AP

title: Why you should think twice before jumping on the AI caricature trend
text:
You upload a photo of yourself, answer a few questions about your interests, and within seconds you have a personalised caricature that you can share on social media.

It is not an original drawing by an artist that you strolled past on the street — it is generated by a machine after it has mined your conversation history and scraped images online.

The viral artificial intelligence (AI) caricature trend has raised privacy and ethical concerns from artists and researchers, who say when something is free, you are the product.

Counting the cost

Last year, the creative sector celebrated the federal government's decision to rule out changing copyright laws to give tech giants free rein to train AI models on creative works.

But for Canberra amateur artist Anne Rowlands, the rise of AI-generated "art" means she has lost work.

Amateur artist Anne Rowlands says she has lost work due to the rise of AI-generated "art". (Supplied: Anne Rowlands)

"They're environmentally destructive as well as being destructive to the creativity of humankind," she said.

Ms Rowlands said she charged between $80 and $160 per commission, which was "pretty cheap" compared to other artists.

"I used to get maybe five or six [commissions] a year," she said.

"Since this AI stuff has gotten to the point where the art is theoretically good enough, as far as people are concerned, all of my commissions have gone."

Anne Rowlands's commissioned work includes illustrations for Dungeons and Dragons and mythical animals such as this dragon cat. (Supplied: Anne Rowlands)

Art is not her main source of income but Ms Rowlands says the widespread adoption of the technology poses a threat to the creative sector.

She noted that the world's biggest comic book convention, the San Diego Comic-Con, had banned AI art from its 2026 show.

"It's just really becoming more mainstream in terms of what people are using it for, not just their own individual use but also for business and events," Ms Rowlands said.

"That's got to be taking a toll on some people's jobs."

Anne Rowlands says people using AI miss out on the creative conversation with artists. (Supplied: Anne Rowlands)

Ms Rowlands was concerned the rise of AI art would diminish creative practice for future generations.

"Artists are going to still do art but … they won't show anyone and that's sort of frightening," she said.

"I have a nephew who I'm helping learn to do art and I see how wonderful it is for him to learn new things from me.

"If he just learned to do it [with] AI instead, he wouldn't have that feeling."

South Australian multimedia artist Luku Kuku said AI video generation was not yet at a stage where it could threaten his work.

But he said people should consider what went into making AI art.

Luku Kuku says he has tinkered with AI but does not use it in his work. (Supplied: Luku Kuku)

"It's built on a plagiarism machine," Kuku said.

"Any time those portraits or caricatures are coming up, if they're looking a certain way because of a certain aesthetic, it's because a whole bunch of other creators' work has been fed into that algorithm to be able to be churned out.

"It's worth considering that this is a large theft of creators' work and aesthetic that is not being properly reckoned with."

Privacy warning

Queensland University of Technology digital communication professor Daniel Angus said image generation technology has greatly improved.

"A lot of energy has gone in to make them far more kind of realistic in terms of the way in which they characterise people, getting rid of things like phantom fingers," he said.

The 2024 Australian Cybercrime Survey found almost three quarters of Australians had used at least one AI app over the space of 12 months. (ABC News: Luke Bowden)

Dr Angus said people should exercise caution when sharing personal data.

"I don't think that enough actually is done, sometimes, by governments to limit how that data can be taken from us and how it's stored and then used," he said.

He urged people to think critically before sharing that "cute caricature".

"Make sure there's no clues or things hidden in that image that might give away otherwise very sensitive information," Dr Angus said.

" The golden rule I always kind of go with is, 'What would happen if this leaked?' "

As experts raise the alarm about potential harms, the federal government plans to launch an AI safety institute in early 2026.

A spokesperson for Federal Minister for Industry and Innovation of Australia, Tim Ayres, said using creators' work for commercial gain without permission was theft.

"The Government is consulting on possible updates to copyright laws to ensure that Australia is prepared for future copyright challenges emerging from AI — while reiterating that this will not include a Text and Data Mining Exception," they said in a statement.

title: 'Game-changer' new suburb announced for inner Sydney
text:
Thousands of foreshore homes are earmarked for a new Sydney suburb along the harbour, but future residents will have a bustling cruise terminal near their doorstep.

The state government on Tuesday confirmed a new housing precinct would be developed on the current site of Glebe Island, a prominent and largely industrial port and logistics hub.

Up to 8,500 homes have been promised for Bays West, the first new suburb to be built in inner Sydney in decades.

A minimum of 10 per cent affordable and essential worker homes were promised as part of the development, with the housing to sit directly above the new Bays West Metro Station currently under construction.

The housing will sit directly above the new Bays West Metro Station currently under construction. (Supplied: NSW Government)

Bays West is located around 4 kilometres west of Sydney's CBD.

While some port activities will cease operations as part of the new housing precinct, White Bay will remain a working, and busy, harbour.

Cruise terminal in close proximity to homes

Up to 8,500 homes have been promised for Bays West, with an artist's impression showing what the new site could look like. (Supplied: NSW Government)

Opposite the future housing precinct is the White Bay Cruise Terminal, which welcomes over 100 cruise ships annually.

It is designed for ships that fit under the Harbour Bridge, such as mid-sized and luxury liners, with mid-sized cruise ships typically carrying between 1,000 and 2,500 passengers.

The cruise terminal — located approximately 2 kilometres away from Bays West — will be retained in its current form, Planning Minister Paul Scully said.

Given the close proximity between the cruise terminal and housing precinct, the government said new shore-power facilities were currently being installed to help reduce noise and emissions.

NSW Premier Chris Minns acknowledged "not everyone will welcome change".

"This is a better use for a prime waterfront land than a concrete works," he said at Tuesday's media conference.

"This is a game-changer for Sydney."

Mr Minns also said in a statement: "If Sydney is going to remain a place young people and families can afford to live in, we have to use well-located land better and plan for the future."

How the housing will look remains undecided, with an international design competition to guide development across the precinct.

Bulk port operations to cease at new suburb site

Bulk port operations will cease on Glebe Island by no later than 2030. (Supplied: NSW Government)

In 2024, the state government reviewed whether the ports at Glebe Island near the Anzac Bride should make way for thousands of new homes, despite the site being a hub for many industries.

As part of the new suburb announcement on Tuesday, Mr Scully confirmed bulk port operations — including cement, gypsum and sugar handling — would cease on Glebe Island by no later than 2030.

The iconic giant silos motorists see on their left as they drive across the Anzac Bridge towards Pyrmont will also be axed.

"They're definitely going … the silos currently generate more revenue from their use as a billboard than what they do from storing materials," Mr Scully told 702 ABC Sydney.

"They're not currently listed on the state register and preliminary investigations have indicated the adaptive reuse of those existing silos wouldn't be feasible."

The Bays Precinct is located 2 kilometres west of Sydney's CBD, in the inner west and Glebe Island. (Supplied: NSW Government)

Mr Scully said the state government would work closely with industry to "minimise impacts", adding that the departing bulk port operations would move to Port Kembla in Wollongong.

"What we are doing to assist with that is investing $270 million in road and other transport upgrades in and around Port Kembla to accommodate those additional movements."

Maritime Union Australia Sydney branch deputy secretary Paul Garrett acknowledged the need for additional housing but said moving bulk port operations was not the solution.

"It's rushed and it's wrong plus we lose a working port, and that's a mistake you can never undo," he told 702 ABC Sydney.

"We're going to be one of the few major ports in the world where we have to go into other ports to bring things in."

Not every port activity is ending however.

Bulk deepwater berths will be retained in White Bay specifically, with working harbour operations across the precinct to also be consolidated mostly into White Bay.

Fireworks for the New Year's Eve and Vivid displays will still take place in the area, with the government vowing to establish a new staging area in White Bay.

Glebe Island has been a pivotal logistics spot for the Sydney New Year's Eve fireworks. (ABC News: Thorsten Joses)

However, Mr Garrett questioned the feasibility of the proposal.

"If you look at the berth space available at White Bay, it's just not there for all this work," he said.

The Sydney Heritage Fleet, which maintains and restores some of Sydney's oldest marine vessels, would also be offered a new premises in White Bay, Mr Scully said.

title: Vice-president of national farming body refuses to resign over 'unacceptable' posts
text:
The vice-president of the National Farmers' Federation (NFF) has refused to step down after the board asked him to reconsider his position.

President Hamish McIntyre said he asked his deputy, WA farmer John Hassell, to consider stepping down after a series of social media posts.

Hamish McIntyre says the board does not have the power to remove elected officials. (ABC News: Callum Flinn)

The posts were on a range of different issues, including asking a question about the safety of women in the rural workforce, migration and the gender of a female Olympic athlete.

Mr Hassell, a Pingelly farmer, was re-elected as NFF vice-president in October last year and was previously the WAFarmers president.

He confirmed to the ABC he was asked to step down, and that he had deleted his X account before reinstating it.

Posts a 'request' for data

The NFF board took action after posts made on February 11, when Mr Hassell made comments online in response to a video by New South Wales farmer Charlie Bloomfield, which called out the sexual harassment of women in agriculture and quoted a 2021 University of New England study.

The study found two thirds of female workers in rural areas had been harassed, which Mr Bloomfield said in an Instagram post it made him "f***ing furious" and urged others to "call it out".

Mr Bloomfield described an incident on his farm between a female and a visiting male truck driver in which the latter was "reaching across her lap … he was generally just making her feel really uncomfortable because he's up in her grill."

John Hassell says he was simply making a request for more information. (X)

"Please provide the source data!" Mr Hassell wrote on the post. Later he said that "having been aware that previous studies were badly flawed I couldn't be bothered watching."

Mr Hassell told the ABC he did not understand why the post asking for data made people "uptight".

"It wasn't a comment. It was a question. It was a request," he said.

The ensuing debate on X also involved Livestock SA chair Gillian Fennell, who posted a series of other similar studies, saying "if you need more data to prove something that every woman knows."

John Hassell defended his request for data on a post about women's safety in the rural workforce. (X)

Livestock SA is affiliated with the National Farmers' Federation via its membership of Primary Producers SA.

NFF board 'disappointed'

Mr McIntyre said he called Mr Hassell as soon as he became aware of the posts.

"He assured me he'd close his accounts, but it was just very disappointing," Mr McIntyre said.

"The comments are unacceptable and the interaction was unacceptable.

" It doesn't align with our code of ethics and we as a board were very disappointed. "

He said it was discussed with the NFF board and a decision was made to ask Mr Hassell to reconsider his elected position.

"And from that discussion with our board, I spoke to him again and said, you know, he should reconsider his position," Mr McIntyre said.

But he admits he has no power to remove Mr Hassell.

"The board doesn't have any legal powers to do any more than that," Mr McIntyre said.

"We're elected and put in these positions by our members, and they then can remove us."

John Hassell was active on the social media platform X. (X)

Process 'grossly unfair'

Mr Hassell has defended his social media posts and his decision to stay on as vice-president.

He said the NFF board did not follow due process and he felt like he was being bullied.

"To me, this is kind of a siege mentality by the board over a comment by someone, which makes me think there's something bigger here at play," Mr Hassell said.

He said deleting his X account was not an admission of guilt.

"I just don't need this backlash over something that's not been said," Mr Hassell said.

"Because of the way this has played out, I've actually reactivated my account because I don't want to lose everything that I had."

Mr McIntyre said he would remain professional while Mr Hassell stayed in the position, but the vice-president admitted the situation was untenable.

He said it made it "really challenging" that a "formal complaint" had not been made and said he thought he was subject to a "witch hunt" and "bullying".

title: RBA says March rate hike a 'live' possibility amid Iran 'supply shock' warning
text:
Reserve Bank governor Michele Bullock says it is too early to know what impact the Middle East war will have on Australia's economy.

With Israel, the United States, and Iran trading deadly missile strikes in recent days, oil tankers in the Strait of Hormuz have been severely disrupted and global oil prices have spiked.

US-Israel attack Iran live updates: For the latest news on the war in the Middle East read our blog.

Economists have warned that Australian petrol prices could rise by 40 cents a litre in coming weeks, after the price of a barrel of Brent crude oil jumped by 9 per cent yesterday.

But Ms Bullock said events were moving rapidly and there were different ways the war could play out, with RBA staff closely monitoring the situation.

Economists warn Middle East war to spike oil The average household could see $14 added to their weekly petrol bill, a prominent economist has warned.

"Events in the Middle East are a timely reminder that in this world of geopolitical uncertainty, things can change quickly," she said at an AFR Business Summit in Sydney early this morning.

"It's too early to say what the impact will be … [RBA] staff will take some time to make sense of what it could mean for inflation here.

"A supply shock could, for example, add to inflation pressures and the potential implications for inflation expectations are something we are very alert to.

"But at the same time, a prolonged impact on energy markets could have adverse effects on global economic activity and result in downward pressure on inflation. It is not obvious how this might play out.

"So as much as I know the public would like more certainty about the direction of interest rates, it would be wrong for us to pretend to have greater certainty than we do."

Michele Bullock said she was not making any prediction for March but every RBA meeting was live. (ABC News: John Gunn)

Every meeting is live: Bullock

Ms Bullock also said the RBA had not ruled out an interest rate rise when it met again on March 16-17, saying "every meeting is live".

Many economists had pegged May as the most likely timing for another rate hike, given the central bank would have received March quarter inflation data before that meeting.

However, the governor emphasised that the RBA board would move rates when required and was not locked into any path.

"If you go back to COVID, when inflation was shooting above 7 per cent, we weren't waiting for quarterly meetings then, we were moving much more quickly," she said.

"Then you recall, when the markets expected us to move in July last year, and we didn't, that caused a lot of angst as well.

"And now they're settled on the fact that we only go every quarter. I would dissuade them from thinking that. I'm not making a prediction about [the] March [meeting], but it will be a live meeting.

"We have inflation at 3.8 per cent headline and we have unemployment at 4.1%, tight.

"The board will be actively looking at whether or not it needs to move more quickly."

Last month, the RBA hiked the official cash rate for the first time in more than two years, taking it to 3.85 per cent.

Unemployment would be higher if RBA had lifted rates more aggressively

Separately, RBA assistant governor (economic) Sarah Hunter delivered a speech overnight in Norway at a central banking conference.

Ms Hunter explained to her global central bank colleagues why the RBA had chosen a deliberate strategy, in recent years, of trying to bring inflation down more slowly to keep unemployment as low as possible.

Bullock calls for patience Michelle Bullock gives more details on how quickly developments affecting monetary policy have moved since the middle of last year.

She said the RBA could have lifted interest rates higher in 2023 and 2024 to bring inflation down faster, but that would have led to much higher unemployment.

Ms Hunter said the RBA had, in fact, run modelling to see what would have happened to unemployment if it had lifted rates as high as those of its central banking peers in recent years.

She said it showed Australia's unemployment rate would now be well above 5 per cent (it is currently 4.1 per cent).

"Unsurprisingly, a sharper increase in interest rates would have reduced the peak level of inflation and increased the speed of subsequent disinflation," Ms Hunter said.

"We estimate that with this counterfactual path, year-ended trimmed mean [underlying] inflation would have reached the midpoint of the target band (2.5 per cent) by 2025.

The RBA modelled what would have happened to inflation if rates had been lifted higher.

"The surprise spike in inflation in late 2025 still occurs, as it is an unexpected shock, with year-ended trimmed mean inflation lifting to be slightly above target at the end of the horizon," she said.

"But the more contractionary policy path would have caused higher unemployment.

"We estimate that the counterfactual strategy would have caused unemployment to overshoot the level consistent with full employment in 2023.

"From then, the labour market would have continued to weaken through 2024 and 2025, peaking at 5.3 per cent in late 2025."

RBA modelling showed the unemployment rate would have peaked above 5 per cent in a higher interest rate scenario.

Ms Hunter said central banks had used different strategies to bring inflation down in recent years to suit their own economic circumstances.

"Some chose to increase policy rates sharply and slow their economy quickly," she said.

"The RBA board chose a more gradual increase and more gradual subsequent policy rate decline, while being very mindful that this strategy relies on inflation expectations remaining anchored."

RBA deputy governor defends central bank from critics RBA deputy governor Andrew Hauser says central banks should not criticise normal government spending choices.

In recent weeks, some economists have been highly critical of the RBA's post-COVID inflation-fighting strategy, with some labelling it a failed experiment.

But Ms Bullock has been reminding people that low unemployment is a good thing.

Last week, she also said she did not think inflation was taking off again, despite the slight rise in prices in the second half of 2025.

However, RBA deputy governor Andrew Hauser said that, given how finely balanced Australia's economy was at the moment, even relatively small demand shocks might lift inflation enough to force monetary policy to respond.

The Australian Bureau of Statistics will publish its December quarter gross domestic product (GDP) figures tomorrow.

title: ‘Won’t be steamrolled’: Eels knock back Storm’s last attempts to do a Lomax deal
text:
Parramatta claims it refused to be “steamrolled” in coming to a resolution with Zac Lomax after knocking back a $750,000 transfer fee offer and a final attempt to offload Ryan Matterson to Melbourne went awry.

The parties still came to an out-of-court settlement on Tuesday, with Melbourne effectively paying $250,000 worth of Parramatta’s legal fees in exchange for Lomax being able to return to the NRL in 2028 – 12 months earlier than the original release agreement that he struck with the Eels.

Sources speaking on the condition of anonymity due to commercial sensitivities have told this masthead the Storm had agreed to also sign Matterson, in a bid to end the impasse.

Informed sources said the Storm’s latest offer was to pay the remainder of Matterson’s 2026 contract – worth approximately $416,000 on the salary cap – as well as a further $300,000 in cash to the blue and golds. Under the proposal, Lomax would have made his Storm debut later in the season, given Melbourne didn’t have the salary cap space required to take Matterson and pay the winger’s full freight.

title: ASX extends losses as airlines, consumer and gold stocks fall
text:
Advertisement

Updated BusinessMarketsShares ASX extends losses as airlines, consumer and gold stocks fall Staff writers Updated March 3, 2026 — 1:36pm ,first published March 3, 2026 — 5:21am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Save this article for later Add articles to your saved list and come back to them anytime. Got it Share A A A

The Australian sharemarket extended its declines at lunchtime as consumer-dependent companies, travel stocks and miners declined amid worries that the Iran war will drag on, clogging the flow of oil, disrupting global transport and making inflation even worse. The S&P/ASX 200 fell 122 points, or 1.3 per cent, to 9078.90 as of 1.30pm AEDT in a broad-based sell-off, with all 11 industry sectors in the red. The bourse had edged higher on Monday. The Aussie dollar traded at US70.98¢ as of 1.31pm AEDT, up 0.1 per cent. Futures contracts for Wall Street’s S&P 500 Index and the Nasdaq 100 also edged lower after the US market erased initial losses to close little changed overnight. Bond yields went up as concerns that spiking energy costs would fan inflation curtailed hopes for rate cuts in the world’s largest economy. Wall Street swung from sharp losses to a tiny gain as investors grapple with the implications of the Iran war. Bloomberg Traders are now pricing in a first US rate cut in September, with bets on a third reduction this year fading. That shift comes on top of global equity markets already unsettled by the billions companies are pouring into artificial intelligence and concerns over the technology’s disruptive impact.

Advertisement

“There are more questions than answers right now,” said Chris Larkin at E*Trade from Morgan Stanley. “A stabilising energy picture could have a positive ripple effect, while concerns about a longer-term disruption could have the opposite.” Gold stocks stepped back some of their gains from Monday as the rally in gold prices moderated. Bullion climbed as much as 0.9 per cent to around $US5360 an ounce, having added more than 3 per cent over the previous four sessions. Related Article Opinion

AI Trump declares war on one of his weapons Stephen Bartholomeusz Senior business columnist Traders weighed the prospect of tighter Fed policy to tame inflation spurred by the conflict in the Middle East, which boosted the US dollar as a safe haven alternative. Northern Star Resources fell 4.2 per cent, Newmont lost 3.4 per cent and Evolution Mining slumped 5.4 per cent. Meanwhile, silver prices fell, sending down South32 - which owns Australia’s biggest silver mine - by 4.6 per cent. Iron ore giants BHP, Fortescue Metals and Rio Tinto were also lower amid the global economic concerns, down 2 per cent, 3.4 per cent and 1.9 per cent, respectively.

Advertisement

Interest rate speculation also weighed on companies dependent on consumer discretionary spending, as higher borrowing costs would leave households with less money to shop. Australia’s interest rate-setting board is “very alert” to the potential implications for inflation expectations from the Middle East conflict and is “well positioned” for a policy response if required, Reserve Bank governor Michele Bullock said at a business summit in Sydney this morning. Wesfarmers, the owner of the Bunnings, Kmart and Officeworks chains, lost 2.9 per cent. Electronics retailer JB Hi-Fi was down 2 per cent and furniture seller Harvey Norman lost 1.7 per cent. The interest-rate-sensitive tech sector also declined, wiping out early gains. Life 360 shares slumped 9.5 per cent even after the family tracking app provider said it had swung into the black last year with $US150.8 million ($210.4 million), boosted by a one-off tax gain. Without that, it made a $US32.5 million profit, up from a $US4.6 million loss in 2024, as sales jumped 32 per cent. The company flagged sales growth of 31 per cent to 39 per cent this year. WiseTech, the nation’s biggest tech concern, dropped 0.7 per cent and AI data centre operator Next DC shed 1.5 per cent.

Advertisement

Airline and travel stocks extended their falls, with Qantas down 2.7 per cent and major Qatar Airways code-share partner Virgin Australia losing 6.4 per cent, as the escalating conflict sent oil prices surging and continues to disrupt flights via the region. Flight Centre lost 1.3 per cent. Oil climbed further as the US and Israel stepped up their war against Iran, while Tehran threatened a full closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Brent rose toward $US79 a barrel, after spiking about 7 per cent overnight, while West Texas Intermediate was near $US72. President Donald Trump said he expected the attacks could last four to five weeks, but “we have the capability to go far longer than that.” At least 11,000 flights to and from Middle Eastern countries have been cancelled, affecting more than 1 million travellers worldwide, according to Cirium, an aviation data firm. Related Article Opinion

Middle East at war Dire straits: The oil industry is facing its worst nightmare Stephen Bartholomeusz Senior business columnist On Wall Street overnight, the S&P 500 fell as much as 1.2 per cent at the start of trading, with cruise lines and airlines leading the way lower. But it quickly erased those losses, in part because past military conflicts haven’t usually created sustained drops for the market, and finished the day with a gain of less than 0.1 per cent.

Advertisement

The Dow Jones Industrial Average dipped 73 points, or 0.1 per cent, and the Nasdaq composite rose 0.4 per cent. Both also came back from steep early losses. Past military conflicts in the Middle East have not caused long-term drops for markets. For this war to knock down Wall Street in a significant and sustained way, the price of oil would perhaps need to jump above $US100 per barrel, according to strategists at Morgan Stanley led by Michael Wilson. Related Article Investment banking I can smell another financial crash coming, says former Goldman Sachs chief That helped the US stock market pare some of its steep opening loss. Morgan Stanley says the S&P 500 has climbed an average of 2 per cent, 6 per cent and 8 per cent in the one, six and 12 months following “geopolitical risk events” historically. That’s going back to the Korean War, which began in 1950, and the 1956 Suez crisis. At the moment, though, fear is still running through Wall Street.

Advertisement

Stocks of airlines were some of the sharpest losers there overnight. Not only do higher oil prices threaten their already big fuel bills, the fighting in the Middle East also closed airports and left travellers stranded. United Airlines fell 2.9 per cent, and American Airlines lost 4.2 per cent. Helping to limit Wall Street’s losses were oil companies. Exxon Mobil climbed 1.1 per cent, and Occidental Petroleum rose 2.1 per cent. Companies that make equipment for the military also strengthened. Lockheed Martin climbed 3.3 per cent, and RTX rallied 4.7 per cent. Palantir Technologies, whose software helps global defence agencies, jumped 5.8 per cent for the biggest gain in the S&P 500. Big Tech stocks also helped to support the market. Nvidia rose 2.9 per cent and was the strongest single force pushing upward on the S&P 500.

title: Why chasing a dollar figure won’t fix your money worries
text:
Advertisement

MoneyPlanning & budgetingMoney psychology Opinion Why chasing a dollar figure won’t fix your money worries Paridhi Jain Money contributor March 3, 2026 — 1:11pm

March 3, 2026 — 1:11pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Save this article for later Add articles to your saved list and come back to them anytime. Got it Share A A A

So many people think there’s a dollar figure that will finally alleviate their financial anxiety. If you can just hustle, control, sacrifice, a little longer until you finally reach that milestone – then, you tell yourself, then you’ll finally feel okay, safe, successful, like you can relax. There is some truth to that. Certainly, you can experience a lot of relief when you finally get debt-free, stop living pay cheque to pay cheque, or hit a significant savings or investment goal. But those are not the scenarios I’m referring to. If you’re trying to overcome your financial anxiety, start by looking at your behaviours. Simon Letch I’m talking about the multiple six-figure earner who is burning himself out to get to that seven-figure net-worth because only then will he will finally feel successful. Or the careful professional with multiple six-figures in investments, who is still avoiding spending on small luxuries out of fear. I’m talking about the early-retirement enthusiast who is diligently squirrelling away every extra dollar into investments, counting down the years until they can finally start living their ‘real’ life. Or those who are financially stable, but are still perpetually postponing their experience of life until a future financial milestone in the name of ambition or responsibility.

Advertisement

However, what I’ve seen in working with my clients, is that while that future milestone may arrive, the experience of life that you thought would come with it, often doesn’t. Here’s why: You create your feelings, not your money Money does not create feelings. It can’t reach into your brain and push the “happy” or “sad” button. Your feelings are internally generated by you. Yes, you respond to your external stimulus emotionally, but still – you are the one generating the experience. If you really want to change your experience of money – you have to break some emotional habits. This distinction is subtle but important. It means you have some power in changing how you feel. It means you don’t have to wait until some future milestone to feel how you want to feel.

Advertisement

You don’t have to wait until you hit a specific net-worth, or salary, or revenue in your business, to feel successful. You don’t have to wait until you pay off your mortgage, hit your ‘early-retirement’ number, or reach a specific savings goal, to feel secure. You can choose to start feeling that now. Editor's pick Opinion

Ask an expert I earn lots, but hate my job. Should I stay for the sake of my bank account? Paridhi Jain Money contributor If your knee-jerk reaction is “but how can I feel that way, without the circumstances to justify it?” Remember – you are responsible for your feelings. You don’t need any permission or circumstance to start feeling the way you want to feel, right now. Is this easier said than done? Yes. Not because it’s not possible – but because you already have a practiced response to your financial situation, which takes time to unlearn and rewire. But this is the key to changing your experience of money. In fact, I would argue this is the only way to start changing your experience of money, because…

Advertisement

Your practiced response to money becomes habitual The ‘rat race’ isn’t just financial – it’s also psychological and emotional. This is the reason so many high-achievers stay stuck in a mode of ‘striving but never arriving’. Your emotional experience, repeated over time, becomes habitual. If your practiced response to your financial situation is dissatisfaction, then that is the emotional habit you will have created. Even when your financial situation changes, even when you finally hit that big goal of yours, it won’t take long before you return to your baseline emotional state. This is why many people have the experience of achieving some progress – an increase in salary, a bonus, achieving a specific milestone, paying off the mortgage – but quickly, their emotional experience does not feel significantly different to before.

Advertisement

If they were stressed about the debt before, they are now stressed about achieving a savings goal. If they were anxious or dissatisfied before the promotion, it’s not long before they feel that way again and are eyeing another pay-increase. Editor's pick Opinion

Big four How $44 and an ‘ambiguous’ email caused a credit score nightmare Victoria Devine Money columnist In fact, you might even unconsciously re-create the old situation because that feels familiar and safe. You see this in lottery winners who, within a few years, end up back in the same situation they were in before. Struggle and lack feel familiar, abundance does not. So, if you really want to change your experience of money – you have to break some emotional habits. You have to change your practiced emotional responses to your financial situation. This is the work most people avoid. It is easier to continue chasing the next number, and telling yourself that ‘when’ you get there, then you will finally feel (insert feeling).

Advertisement

Because as long as that milestone is in the future, you can avoid the real question: why do you need a specific number to justify how you want to feel – instead of choosing to feel that now? Paridhi Jain is founder of SkilledSmart, which helps adults learn to manage, save and invest money through financial education courses and classes. Advice given in this article is general in nature and not intended to influence readers’ decisions about investing or financial products. They should always seek their own professional advice that takes into account their own personal circumstances before making any financial decisions.
